filer: Louisiana Public Service CommissionMississippi Public Service Commission
source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5183__Louisiana-Public-Service-CommissionMississippi-Pub/RM26-4 COMMENTS OF THE LOUISIANA AND MISSISSIPPI COMMISSIONS.txt
overall_summary: The Louisiana and Mississippi Public Service Commissions oppose broad federal jurisdiction over large load interconnection, arguing the ANOPR invades exclusive state authority over resource adequacy, retail rates, and distribution. They contend no problem exists in MISO and SPP regions where state processes have delivered timely interconnections, and they ask FERC to raise the 20 MW threshold, limit any rule to areas with demonstrated delays, and preserve state flexibility.

quotes.text: the proposed implementation of that goal raises jurisdictional, reliability, resource adequacy, transparency, planning, and cost allocation concerns
quotes.concern: ANOPR implementation raises multiple concerns beyond its stated goal

quotes.text: Louisiana and Mississippi have not had any issues associated with the delay of the interconnection of large loads, and FERC should be mindful that any rules adopted could unintentionally result in delays, instead of acceleration of the desired interconnections
quotes.concern: No interconnection delay problem exists in Louisiana or Mississippi; rule could backfire

quotes.text: The ANOPR may be a solution in search of a problem.
quotes.concern: Filers question whether a federal rule is needed at all

quotes.text: the rule should not apply to any transmission owner or transmission provider in the absence of a demonstration that large load interconnections are being met with significant delays
quotes.concern: Rule should be conditioned on a showing of actual delays

quotes.text: The ANOPR asserts, without offering any evidence that large load interconnection is unreasonable incumbered, that federal jurisdiction is needed to timely interconnect end-use retail customers, which is traditionally within the purview of retail regulators.
quotes.concern: ANOPR lacks evidentiary basis for asserting federal jurisdiction over retail interconnection

quotes.text: the interconnection process accomplishes little without actions to evaluate and approve any generation, distribution and intrastate transmission facilities needed to maintain reliability and resource adequacy – all matters that are within the exclusive jurisdiction of retail regulators
quotes.concern: Interconnection alone is insufficient without state-controlled resource adequacy and distribution approvals

quotes.text: This expansion of federal jurisdiction is not needed in MISO South and SPP regions, the members of which mainly consist of vertically integrated utilities and cooperatives, and it is not needed in Louisiana and Mississippi.
quotes.concern: Federal jurisdiction expansion is unnecessary in MISO South and SPP

quotes.text: Even if the FERC technically is granted jurisdiction over large load interconnections to the interstate transmission system, it should refrain from exercising that jurisdiction in locations where it is not needed under principles of cooperative federalism.
quotes.concern: Cooperative federalism counsels restraint even where FERC has technical jurisdiction

quotes.text: any rules adopted should not be a one-size-fits-all proposition. Rules must take into account regional differences. State and RTO processes that have worked well should be accommodated and not changed. Flexibility is crucial to meeting the needs of new large loads and of the affected states.
quotes.concern: Rule must be regionally flexible and accommodate successful existing processes

quotes.text: Giving FERC jurisdiction over the load interconnection as if it were a standalone issue would hamper the efficient planning and development of these projects.
quotes.concern: Siloing load interconnection under FERC disrupts integrated state planning

quotes.text: the states have the authority and duty to develop protective measures in approving facilities needed to serve large loads, including measures designed to fairly allocate the costs and to protect reliability so that retail ratepayers are not unfairly burdened
quotes.concern: States must retain authority to protect ratepayers from cost shifts

quotes.text: The Louisiana and Mississippi Commissions suggest that 20 MW is too low and will result in FERC exercising interconnection jurisdiction over hundreds and thousands of new loads, far beyond FERC's capabilities of reasonably addressing.
quotes.concern: 20 MW threshold is too low and will overload FERC capacity

quotes.text: The threshold should be far higher and set to only address larger loads that are shown to be affected by interconnection delays.
quotes.concern: Threshold should be raised and tied to demonstrated delay

bins.name: State jurisdiction must be preserved
bins.description: The commissions argue the ANOPR intrudes on exclusive state authority over retail load interconnection, resource adequacy, distribution, and planning. They contend FERC has never before claimed jurisdiction over retail load interconnections and should not do so here, especially without evidence of a problem. Even where technical federal jurisdiction exists, cooperative federalism requires restraint.

bins.name: 20 MW threshold too low
bins.description: The 20 MW threshold would sweep in hundreds of thousands of loads, overwhelming FERC. The commissions urge a much higher threshold limited to loads where interconnection delays have actually been demonstrated.

bins.name: Ratepayer cost protection at risk
bins.description: States have both the authority and the duty to ensure that the costs of serving large loads do not fall unfairly on retail ratepayers. Federal preemption of load interconnection removes that protective layer.

bins.name: Cost allocation concerns for retail customers
bins.description: The filing flags cost allocation as a core concern, warning that federal rules could impose undue costs on retail consumers and undermine state mechanisms that fairly distribute infrastructure costs.

bins.name: Integrated planning cannot be siloed
bins.description: The commissions argue that load interconnection cannot be treated as a standalone issue separate from generation, distribution, and transmission planning. A FERC-only interconnection study process disrupts the bottom-up integrated planning that incumbent utilities and states currently perform together.

bins.name: Regional flexibility required
bins.description: The commissions support a flexible approach that accommodates regional differences and does not override state processes that are already working. They ask that any rule apply only where delays have been demonstrated and that states retain the ability to manage interconnections under their own successful procedures.

bins.name: MISO South process works
bins.description: Louisiana and Mississippi participate in MISO South through vertically integrated utilities and cooperatives. The commissions say no interconnection delays exist in this region and cite the approval of the $27 billion Meta data center project in under nine months as proof.

bins.name: SPP region needs no federal override
bins.description: The commissions also operate in the SPP footprint and contend the same argument applies: no demonstrated delays, so no federal rule is warranted.
